title: .STOMATOLOGICAL PREPARATIONS
pathophysiology: Dental caries, gingivitis, and oral candidiasis are primary oral pathologies. Caries develops when acidogenic bacteria (Streptococcus mutans) metabolize dietary carbohydrates to lactic acid, demineralizing tooth enamel. Gingivitis involves bacterial biofilm formation triggering inflammatory cascade (IL-1, TNF-α). Oral candidiasis occurs when Candida albicans overgrowth disrupts normal oral flora, typically in immunocompromised states or antibiotic use.
pharmacology: Fluoride strengthens enamel via fluorapatite formation, increasing acid resistance. Antimicrobials (chlorhexidine, hexetidine) disrupt bacterial cell membranes. Antifungals (clotrimazole, miconazole) inhibit fungal ergosterol synthesis. Anti-inflammatory agents reduce gingival edema and bleeding.
dispensing_guidance: Fluoride products: counsel on spitting out after use (toxicity risk in children <6 yrs). Antifungals: full course needed (7-14 days) even if symptoms resolve. Chlorhexidine: causes staining; avoid in nursing mothers (absorption into breast milk). Patient education on plaque removal essential for compliance.

title: DRUGS FOR ACID RELATED DISORDERS
pathophysiology: GERD, peptic ulcers, and Zollinger-Ellison syndrome result from excessive gastric acid. Normal gastric pH 1.5-2 protects against pathogens but damages mucosa if protective barriers fail. H. pylori infection disrupts mucin layer. Acid causes protein denaturation, epithelial necrosis, and bleeding. NSAIDs inhibit cytoprotective prostaglandins, increasing ulcer risk.
pharmacology: Antacids (Al/Mg hydroxides): neutralize acid via buffering, onset 5 min, duration 30-60 min. H2-blockers (cimetidine, famotidine): block histamine-H2 receptors on parietal cells, reducing acid 60-70%. PPIs (omeprazole, pantoprazole): irreversibly inhibit H+/K+-ATPase, reducing acid 90%+, onset 3-5 days. Mucoprotective agents (sucralfate): polymerize in acidic pH to coat ulcer base.
dispensing_guidance: Antacids: take 1-3 hours after meals; separate from other drugs by 2 hrs (absorption interference). H2-blockers: optimal at bedtime or before largest meal. PPIs: take 30-60 min before food, swallow whole (enteric coating); counsel 4-8 week healing period. Avoid NSAIDs during treatment. Monitor long-term PPI use for B12/magnesium depletion.

title: DRUGS FOR FUNCTIONAL BOWEL  DISORDERS
pathophysiology: IBS, colitis, and GI spasm involve smooth muscle hypercontractility. Acetylcholine released from parasympathetic neurons binds muscarinic receptors on smooth muscle, triggering Ca2+ influx and contraction. Dysregulation causes cramping, diarrhea, and abdominal pain.
pharmacology: Anticholinergics (scopolamine, dicyclomine): block muscarinic-M3 receptors on smooth muscle, preventing acetylcholine-induced contraction. Onset 30-60 min. Antispasmodics (mebeverine, alverine): direct smooth muscle relaxation via calcium channel blockade.
dispensing_guidance: Anticholinergics: counsel on dry mouth, urinary retention, blurred vision; avoid in glaucoma/BPH. Take 30-60 min before meals. Contraindicated in myasthenia gravis, tachycardia. Mebeverine: effective only in IBS (not organic disease); full trial 2-3 weeks needed.

title: ANTIEMETICS AND ANTINAUSEANTS
pathophysiology: Nausea/vomiting triggered via chemoreceptor trigger zone (dopamine, serotonin, histamine, NK1 receptors), vestibular system (histamine), or vagal afferents (chemotherapy, gastroenteritis). Vomiting center in medulla integrates signals. Uncontrolled emesis causes electrolyte loss, dehydration, aspiration risk.
pharmacology: Antihistamines (promethazine, cinnarizine): block H1 receptors in vomiting center; anticholinergic properties; cross BBB. 5-HT3 antagonists (ondansetron, granisetron): block serotonin receptors in chemoreceptor trigger zone; highly effective for chemotherapy/post-op nausea. Metoclopramide: D2 antagonist + 5-HT4 agonist; prokinetic effect via acetylcholine sensitization. NK1 antagonists: block substance P.
dispensing_guidance: Metoclopramide: 30 min before meals; risk of tardive dyskinesia with prolonged use (>3 months). Ondansetron: given before chemotherapy/surgery; IV/oral forms. Anticholinergics: avoid in elderly (confusion, retention). Counsel on drowsiness/dizziness with antihistamines.

title: BILE AND LIVER THERAPY
pathophysiology: Cholestasis (intrahepatic/extrahepatic) leads to bile acid accumulation, causing pruritus, malabsorption, and hepatocyte injury. Hyperlipidemia (familial or acquired) increases atherosclerosis risk via LDL oxidation and foam cell formation. Gallstones form from cholesterol supersaturation.
pharmacology: Bile acids (ursodeoxycholic acid): reduce cholesterol saturation in bile, promote cholesterol dissolution in gallstones; also reduce intestinal cholesterol absorption. Statins: inhibit HMG-CoA reductase (rate-limiting step in cholesterol synthesis); reduce LDL 30-50%, raise HDL. Fibrates: activate PPAR-α, increase lipoprotein lipase, lower triglycerides 40-50%. Ezetimibe: blocks NPC1L1 cholesterol transporter in intestine.
dispensing_guidance: Ursodeoxycholic acid: 2-3 years for stone dissolution; monitor with ultrasound. Statins: take at bedtime (max HMG-CoA activity at night); monitor LFTs + creatine kinase (myopathy risk). Fibrates + statins = myopathy risk (monitor). Ezetimibe: can combine with statins; separate dosing by 2 hours from bile acid sequestrants.

title: DRUGS FOR CONSTIPATION
pathophysiology: Constipation results from slow colonic transit, inadequate fluid intake, or weak defecation reflexes. Severe constipation causes fecal impaction, bowel obstruction, or megacolon. Long-term straining increases hemorrhoid/diverticular disease risk.
pharmacology: Bulk laxatives (psyllium, methylcellulose): absorb water, increase stool volume and trigger reflex. Onset 12-72 hrs. Osmotic laxatives (lactulose, polyethylene glycol): retain water in lumen via osmosis; lactulose metabolized to SCFA, acidifying colon (inhibits ammonia reabsorption—useful in hepatic encephalopathy). Stimulant laxatives (bisacodyl, senna): activate myenteric plexus via altered electrolyte transport; onset 6-12 hrs. Stool softeners (docusate): reduce surface tension, improve water penetration.
dispensing_guidance: Bulk laxatives: require 8-10 cups water daily; ineffective if dehydrated. Lactulose: titrate to 2-3 soft stools/day; can cause bloating/flatulence (fermentation). Bisacodyl: avoid >1 week (mucosal damage risk). PEG: mix powder with 4-8 oz liquid; used pre-operatively. Docusate alone ineffective; combine with other agents.

title: DIGESTIVES INCLUDING ENZYMES
pathophysiology: Pancreatic insufficiency (CF, chronic pancreatitis, post-gastrectomy): inadequate lipase, amylase, protease → steatorrhea, malabsorption, weight loss. Enzyme deficiency causes nutrient loss, fat-soluble vitamin deficiency.
pharmacology: Pancreatic enzyme supplements (pancreatin, pancrelipase): contain lipase, amylase, protease extracted from porcine pancreas. Replace deficient endogenous enzymes; enteric-coated formulations survive stomach acid.
dispensing_guidance: Dose titrated to clinical response (stools/steatorrhea). Take with meals. Enteric-coated forms MUST NOT be crushed (acid inactivation). Monitor for efficacy (weight gain, reduction in steatorrhea). Fish-oil supplements may help absorption.

title: DRUGS USED IN DIABETES
pathophysiology: Type 2 DM: pancreatic β-cell dysfunction + insulin resistance. Hyperglycemia causes non-enzymatic glycation of proteins → microvascular (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) and macrovascular (MI, stroke) complications. Osmotic hyperglycemia causes polyuria/polydipsia. Ketoacidosis in Type 1 DM when insulin absent.
pharmacology: Insulins: exogenous insulin replaces deficient endogenous; rapid (lispro, aspart), NPH (intermediate), basal (glargine, detemir). Mechanism: glucose uptake via GLUT4 translocators; glycogen/protein synthesis activation. Metformin: ↓ hepatic gluconeogenesis via AMP activation; ↑ insulin sensitivity. Sulfonylureas (glyburide, glipizide): K-ATP channel closure on β-cells → insulin release. Dipeptidyl peptidase-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin): ↓ GLP-1 breakdown → ↑ incretin effect. SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin): block renal glucose reabsorption → increased urinary glucose excretion. GLP-1 agonists: as above (A08).
dispensing_guidance: Insulin: teach injection technique, site rotation (prevent lipohypertrophy), storage (refrigerate; room temp max 28 days), hypoglycemia recognition (tremor, sweating, confusion—treat with 15g fast carbs). Metformin: take with meals (GI upset); monitor renal function (eGFR <30 = CI); risk of lactic acidosis (rare). Sulfonylureas: hypoglycemia risk; counsel on missed meals. SGLT2i: monitor for DKA (rare), yeast infections, UTIs. Dose insulin based on pre/post-meal glucose log.

title: VITAMINS
pathophysiology: Micronutrient deficiencies cause specific pathology: iron → anemia; B12/folate → megaloblastic anemia + neuropathy; vitamin D → osteomalacia, rickets; vitamin C → scurvy; vitamin A → blindness; iodine → goiter, cretinism.
pharmacology: Iron: divalent form (Fe2+) absorbed in duodenum via DMT1 transporter; heme iron (meat) better absorbed than non-heme (plants). Vitamin B12: cobalamin cofactor for DNA synthesis; intrinsic factor required for absorption. Folate: methyl-tetrahydrofolate cofactor for 1-C transfer. Vitamin D: converts to calcitriol (active form) in kidneys → intestinal Ca absorption. Calcium: structural mineral; neuromuscular signaling.
dispensing_guidance: Iron: take on empty stomach (enhanced absorption) or with vitamin C; counsel on black stools, constipation, GI upset. Separate iron from PPIs by 2+ hours (reduced absorption). IM B12 if pernicious anemia or absorption defect. Folate: essential in pregnancy (neural tube defect prevention). Vitamin D: higher doses needed in malabsorption (post-gastrectomy). Counsel on over-supplementation risks (hypervitaminosis A toxicity, hypercalcemia).

title: ANTITHROMBOTICS AGENTS
pathophysiology: Thrombosis forms via Virchow's triad: vessel damage (atherosclerosis, surgery), stasis (AF, immobility), and hypercoagulability (cancer, thrombophilia). Platelet aggregation → fibrin mesh formation → vessel occlusion. Arterial thrombosis (MI, stroke) vs. venous thrombosis (DVT, PE).
pharmacology: Antiplatelet drugs: Aspirin → irreversible COX inhibition → ↓ TXA2 (platelet aggregation). P2Y12 inhibitors (clopidogrel, ticagrelor) → block ADP-induced platelet activation. GPIIb/IIIa inhibitors (abciximab) → block fibrinogen bridge. Anticoagulants: Unfractionated heparin (UFH) → potentiates antithrombin III → Factors IIa, Xa inhibition (rapid; IV/SC). Low-molecular-weight heparins (LMWH, enoxaparin) → selective Xa inhibition; SC, longer half-life. Warfarin → vitamin K antagonist → ↓ Factors II, VII, IX, X synthesis (slow; requires INR monitoring). DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran) → direct Xa/IIa inhibition; oral; fixed dosing; no monitoring.
dispensing_guidance: Aspirin: GI upset (take with food); bleeding risk (hold 5-7 days pre-op). Clopidogrel: irreversible effect (≥5 days before surgery). UFH: monitor aPTT (therapeutic 1.5-2.5x baseline); risk of HIT (thrombocytopenia + thrombosis). LMWH: fixed dosing based on weight; monitor anti-Xa if renal impairment (CrCl <30). Warfarin: counsels INR checks (target 2-3 usually), dietary vitamin K consistency, many drug interactions. DOACs: contraindicated in mechanical prosthetic valves; monitor renal function.

title: ANTIHEMORHAGIC DRUGS
pathophysiology: Bleeding disorders result from coagulation factor deficiency (hemophilia), thrombocytopenia, or platelet dysfunction. Excessive bleeding prevents clot formation; inadequate hemostasis → hemorrhage, anemia.
pharmacology: Coagulation factor replacements (Factor VIII for hemophilia A, Factor IX for hemophilia B): exogenous factors restore coagulation cascade. Vitamin K (phytonadione): cofactor for carboxylation of Factors II, VII, IX, X; reverses warfarin effect. Fresh frozen plasma (FFP): contains all factors. Tranexamic acid: plasmin inhibitor → reduced fibrinolysis → ↓ bleeding.
dispensing_guidance: Factor concentrates: slow infusion (anaphylaxis risk); monitor for inhibitor development (frequent dosing). Vitamin K: IM preferred (faster than PO; IV risks anaphylaxis). FFP: ABO-compatible; use in emergencies only (volume overload risk). Tranexamic acid: effective for heavy menstrual bleeding, post-surgical bleeding; contraindicated in thromboembolic disease.

title: CARDIAC THERAPY
pathophysiology: Heart failure: decreased cardiac contractility (↓ CO) triggers compensatory mechanisms (SNS, RAAS, fluid retention) that worsen congestion. Systolic HF: weak contraction. Diastolic HF: stiff ventricle → impaired filling.
pharmacology: Cardiac glycosides (digoxin): inhibit Na+/K+-ATPase → ↑ intracellular Na+ → reduced Na+/Ca2+ exchanger activity → ↑ intracellular Ca2+ → enhanced contraction. Also vagomimetic (↓ conduction). Inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone): β1 agonism (dobutamine) or PDE3 inhibition (milrinone) → ↑ cAMP → ↑ contractility + vasodilation.
dispensing_guidance: Digoxin: narrow therapeutic window (0.5-2 ng/mL); toxicity → arrhythmias, GI upset, visual disturbances. Monitor renal function (accumulates if CrCl <30). Many drug interactions (amiodarone, verapamil increase levels). Baseline EKG needed. Inotropes: IV only; hospital use; monitor BP, HR, for ischemia.

title: ANTIHYPERTENSIVE DRUGS
pathophysiology: Hypertension (>140/90 mmHg) causes vessel remodeling via increased wall stress → smooth muscle hypertrophy → reduced lumen. Endothelial dysfunction → reduced NO → vasoconstriction. Chronic HTN causes LVH, stroke, MI, renal disease.
pharmacology: ACE inhibitors (enalapril, lisinopril): block ACE → ↓ angiotensin II (potent vasoconstrictor) + ↑ bradykinin. ARBs (losartan, valsartan): AT1 receptor antagonism → similar effect without bradykinin buildup. Beta-blockers (metoprolol, atenolol): β1 antagonism → ↓ HR, contractility, renin release. Calcium channel blockers (amlodipine, diltiazem): ↓ vascular smooth muscle Ca influx → vasodilation. Thiazide diuretics (HCTZ, chlorthalidone): ↓ blood volume + vasodilation; hypokalemia/hyperglycemia risk. Alpha-blockers (doxazosin): α1 antagonism → vasodilation.
dispensing_guidance: ACEi: counsel on dry cough (bradykinin; 10-20% incidence). Monitor K+ (hyperkalemia risk). ARBs: no cough; safer in pregnancy than ACEi (teratogenic). Beta-blockers: taper gradually (rebound HTN/MI if stopped abruptly); avoid in asthma/COPD. CCBs: grapefruit interaction (↑ levels); ankle edema common. Thiazides: monitor glucose, lipids, K+, uric acid. Start low dose; combine agents for synergy.

title: DIURETIC DRUGS
pathophysiology: Edema (pulmonary, peripheral) or fluid overload in HF, renal disease, cirrhosis results from sodium retention + water follows osmotically.
pharmacology: Loop diuretics (furosemide): inhibit Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in loop of Henle → ↑ Na excretion (potent; onset 1 hr); high K+ wasting. Thiazides (HCTZ): distal tubule Na-Cl inhibition (moderate potency; 6-12 hr onset). K-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, amiloride): aldosterone antagonism or K channel blockade → minimal K wasting. Osmotic diuretics (mannitol): osmotic effect → water excretion (used for cerebral edema, acute kidney injury prevention).
dispensing_guidance: Loop diuretics: check daily weights (goal 1-2 lbs/day loss); K+ monitoring essential. Doses escalate with fluid retention. Thiazides: monitor for electrolyte derangements (hyponatremia, hypokalemia). K-sparing: contraindicated in renal impairment (hyperkalemia risk); monitor K+ closely. Osmotic: IV administration; risk of hyperkalemia, dehydration if overused.

title: PERIPHERAL VASODILATORS
pathophysiology: Peripheral arterial disease: atherosclerotic occlusion → reduced blood flow → tissue ischemia, claudication (leg pain with exercise).
pharmacology: Phosphodiesterase inhibitors (pentoxifylline): ↓ PDE → ↑ cAMP → improved RBC deformability + reduced platelet aggregation + vasodilation. Prostaglandins (misoprostol, iloprost): vasodilation + antiplatelet effect. Nitrates (isosorbide dinitrate): convert to NO → vasodilation; mechanism: SNP → guanylate cyclase activation → ↑ cGMP.
dispensing_guidance: Pentoxifylline: modest efficacy; requires 8-12 week trial. Nitrates: tolerance develops (24 hr dosing with 12 hr nitrate-free period); headache common (usually subsides). Counsel on regular exercise (most important).

title: VASOPROTECTIVE DRUGS
pathophysiology: Venous insufficiency: valve incompetence → retrograde flow → venous hypertension → vein dilation, varicosities. Stasis → thrombosis risk.
pharmacology: Flavonoids (diosmin, hesperidin): improve venous tone + reduce capillary permeability → reduced edema, pain. Venotonics (aescin): capillary stabilization. These agents are weak; primary treatment is compression therapy.
dispensing_guidance: Compression therapy 1st-line (better efficacy than drugs). Flavonoids: adjunctive; require 4-8 weeks trial. Surgery/ablation if complications (ulcers, thrombosis).

title: AGENTS ACTING ON THE RENIN-ANGIOTENSIN SYSTEM
ANTIFUNGAL FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE
pathophysiology: Fungal infections (tinea, candidiasis) result from dermatophyte or yeast colonization. Warm, moist environments (groins, feet) favor growth. Immunosuppression (HIV, diabetes) enables systemic infection.
pharmacology: Azoles (miconazole, clotrimazole, ketoconazole): inhibit lanosterol 14α-demethylase → ↓ ergosterol synthesis → fungal cell death. Topical = fungistatic; systemic = fungicidal. Polyenes (amphotericin B, nystatin): bind ergosterol → pore formation → cell lysis (fungicidal). Allylamines (terbinafine): inhibit squalene epoxidase → ↓ ergosterol precursors.
dispensing_guidance: Topical azoles: apply BID-TID x 2-4 weeks (depend on extent); continue 1-2 weeks after lesions disappear (prevent relapse). Nystatin: apply QID; risk of contact dermatitis. Terbinafine: PO for nail infections (12 weeks); monitor LFTs. Systemic azoles: monitor LFTs (hepatotoxicity risk).

title: EMOLLIENTS AND PROTECTANTS
pathophysiology: Dry skin (xerosis) results from reduced stratum corneum hydration. Barrier dysfunction → increased TEWL, irritation, eczema.
pharmacology: Emollients: occlusive oils/waxes (petrolatum, lanolin) + humectants (glycerin, urea) → restore hydration + reduce TEWL. Protectants (zinc oxide, calamine): physical barrier + mild anti-inflammatory.
dispensing_guidance: Apply to damp skin for better hydration. Recommend daily use; cheap and safe. Avoid in infections (occlusion worsens bacterial growth).

title: TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS
pathophysiology: Chronic wounds (ulcers, burns) fail to heal due to impaired angiogenesis, inadequate matrix remodeling, or persistent infection.
pharmacology: Growth factors (PDGF, FGF): promote fibroblast proliferation + angiogenesis. Proteolytic enzymes (collagenase, papain): debride necrotic tissue. Antimicrobials (iodine, silver): prevent infection.
dispensing_guidance: Enzymatic debridement: remove eschar 1st (impedes absorption). Apply to wound only (avoid surrounding skin—chemical burn). Regular dressing changes. Gauge effectiveness via healing rate; switch if no improvement in 4 weeks.

title: ANTIPRURITICS DRUGS
pathophysiology: Pruritus (itching) mediated by histamine release from mast cells (allergic) or neuropathic mechanisms (chronic itch-scratch cycle).
pharmacology: Antihistamines (diphenhydramine, hydroxyzine): H1 antagonism → reduced itch sensation. Topical anesthetics (lidocaine, benzocaine): block nociceptors. Corticosteroids (topical): ↓ inflammation → reduced cytokine release.
dispensing_guidance: Antihistamines: sedating types (diphenhydramine) at night; counsel on drowsiness. Avoid prolonged topical anesthetics (skin sensitization). Low-potency steroids for face/intertriginous areas; higher potency for thickened areas (palms, soles).

title: ANTIPSORIATIC DRUGS
pathophysiology: Psoriasis: Th17-mediated autoimmunity → keratinocyte hyperproliferation (normal 28 days → 3-4 days) → scaling plaques. Eczema: impaired skin barrier + Th2 inflammation → itching, erythema.
pharmacology: Corticosteroids: ↓ T-cell infiltration + cytokine production. Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus): block NFAT → T-cell inactivation (steroid-sparing). Retinoids (tretinoin, acitretin): normalize keratinocyte differentiation. Vitamin D analogs (calcipotriene): ↓ keratinocyte proliferation + ↑ differentiation. Biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-17 antagonists): systemic immunosuppression.
dispensing_guidance: Topical steroids: taper gradually (rebound flare); don't use on face long-term (atrophy). Calcineurin inhibitors: safer for face; no atrophy risk. Retinoids: teratogenic (pregnancy CI); photosensitivity (sunscreen required). Methotrexate (oral): slow response (6-12 weeks); monitor CBC, LFTs. Biologics: expensive; reserved for severe disease.

title: ANTIBIOTICS AND CHEMOTHERAPEUTICS  FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE
pathophysiology: Bacterial skin infections (impetigo, folliculitis, cellulitis) from Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes penetrating compromised skin.
pharmacology: Beta-lactams (cephalexin, amoxicillin): cell wall synthesis inhibition. Macrolides (erythromycin): protein synthesis inhibition. Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin): DNA gyrase inhibition. Topical antibiotics (mupirocin, bacitracin): local coverage without systemic absorption.
dispensing_guidance: Mupirocin: BID-TID x 7-10 days; apply to lesions only (antibiotic stewardship). For impetigo limited to localized area. Systemic antibiotics if spreading or deep involvement. Cover with dressing (infection control).

title: TOPICAL DERMATOLOGICAL CORTICOSTEROIDS
pathophysiology: Inflammatory skin conditions (contact dermatitis, allergic reactions) involve mast cell degranulation → histamine → erythema, edema, pruritus.
pharmacology: Corticosteroids: reduce phospholipase A2 → ↓ inflammatory mediators (prostaglandins, leukotrienes); block IL-2 production → T-cell inhibition. Potency varies by formulation (ointment > cream > lotion).
dispensing_guidance: Classify by potency: Class I-II (very high) reserved for thick areas (palms, soles), never face. Class V-VII (low) for face, intertriginous areas, children. Ointments for dry areas; creams for weeping lesions; lotions for hairy areas. Taper after 2 weeks (rebound flare with abrupt discontinuation). Monitor for atrophy (thin, shiny skin), striae.

title: ANTISEPTICS AND DESINFECTANTS
pathophysiology: Microbial contamination of wounds/skin requires reduction in bacterial load pre-operatively and for wound care.
pharmacology: Chlorhexidine: binds bacterial membranes → disruption + leakage. Iodine: oxidative damage to proteins, nucleic acids. Alcohol: protein denaturation. Hydrogen peroxide: oxidative damage.
dispensing_guidance: Chlorhexidine: safe for large areas; persistent activity (residual effect). Iodine: allergic reaction risk; assess allergy history. Alcohol: avoid in open wounds (painful; tissue damage). H2O2: foaming action aids debris removal; don't use in closed wounds (gas embolism risk).

title: OTHER DERMATOLOGICAL DRUGS
pathophysiology: Vulvovaginitis: candidiasis (overgrowth in antibiotics/diabetes), bacterial vaginosis (Gardnerella, anaerobes displace Lactobacillus), trichomoniasis (protozoan). Presents with discharge, odor, pruritus.
pharmacology: Antifungals (miconazole, clotrimazole): as D01. Antibacterials (metronidazole, clindamycin): nucleic acid synthesis inhibition. Antiprotozoan (metronidazole): disrupts redox balance → cell death.
dispensing_guidance: Vaginal formulations: insert high in vagina at bedtime. Metronidazole: metallic taste, disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol (avoid). Full course essential even if symptoms resolve (recurrence risk). Partner treatment for trichomoniasis. Confirm diagnosis (wet mount) before treatment (antibiotics promote resistance).

title: OTHER GYNECOLOGICAL  DRUGS
pathophysiology: Menorrhagia (heavy menstrual bleeding): abnormal endometrial hemostasis, structural pathology (fibroids, adenomyosis), or coagulopathy.
pharmacology: NSAIDs (mefenamic acid): reduce prostaglandin synthesis → ↓ endometrial bleeding. Tranexamic acid: plasmin inhibition → ↓ fibrinolysis. Hormonal agents (oral contraceptives, levonorgestrel IUD): stabilize endometrium.
dispensing_guidance: Mefenamic acid: start on 1st day of menses; 3-5 days duration. Tranexamic acid: during menses only (not for prophylaxis). Hormonal IUD: reduces bleeding 50-90% but requires insertion skills.

title: SEX HORMONE  AND MODULATORS OF THE SEX HORMONE
pathophysiology: Hormone deficiency/imbalance: estrogen deficiency (menopause, hypogonadism) causes vasomotor symptoms, bone loss, vaginal atrophy. Testosterone deficiency (hypogonadism) causes sexual dysfunction, fatigue, muscle loss.
pharmacology: Estrogens (estradiol): nuclear receptor activation → target gene expression (bone maintenance, lipid metabolism). Progestins: endometrial stabilization (prevent hyperplasia if no hysterectomy). Testosterone: androgen receptor activation → virilization, sexual function. SERMs (tamoxifen, raloxifene): tissue-selective agonism/antagonism (bone agonism, breast antagonism).
dispensing_guidance: HRT: individualized benefit-risk (cardiovascular, cancer risk vs. symptom relief). Transdermal preferred if smoking (avoid first-pass thrombosis). Progestin needed if uterus present (endometrial cancer prevention). Testosterone: teratogenic (pregnancy CI); virilizing. Monitor bone density on HRT + cessation (bone loss accelerates).

title: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES
pathophysiology: Anterior pituitary hormone deficiency: GH deficiency → short stature, metabolic dysfunction. TSH deficiency → secondary hypothyroidism. ACTH deficiency → adrenal insufficiency. Prolactin excess (prolactinoma) → galactorrhea, hypogonadism.
pharmacology: GH (somatropin): IGF-1 synthesis → anabolic effects (bone, muscle, metabolism). GnRH agonists (leuprolide): initial agonism → downregulation (↓ LH/FSH). ACTH (cosyntropin): stimulates adrenal cortex → cortisol production (diagnostic). Vasopressin (desmopressin): V2 receptor → aquaporin-2 expression → water reabsorption.
dispensing_guidance: GH: subcutaneous injection; requires growth monitoring (children) or metabolic assessment (adults). Leuprolide: monthly/quarterly IM; flare symptoms initially (↑ testosterone before suppression—add antagonist). Desmopressin: intranasal for central diabetes insipidus; adjust to avoid hyponatremia.

title: SYSTEMIC CORTICOSTEROIDS
pathophysiology: Adrenal insufficiency (primary or secondary): inadequate cortisol production → hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, shock. Autoimmune/inflammatory diseases: Th1/Th17-mediated pathology.
pharmacology: Glucocorticoids (prednisone, dexamethasone, methylprednisolone): GR activation in lymphocytes → ↓ IL-2, TNF-α, IFN-γ. Vasculitis (endothelial stabilization via ↓ ICAM-1). Mineralocorticoid effect (aldosterone-like) varies by agent (dexamethasone = minimal, prednisone = moderate).
dispensing_guidance: Replacement therapy: morning dosing (mimics circadian cortisol peak). Inflammatory/autoimmune: higher doses; taper gradually (adrenal suppression). Monitor blood glucose, bone density, infections (immunosuppression). Long-term: prophylaxis for osteoporosis (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonates). Counsel on stress dosing (↑ dose before surgery/illness).

title: THYROID THERAPY
pathophysiology: Hypothyroidism (autoimmune/iodine deficiency): ↓ thyroid hormone → ↓ metabolic rate, cold intolerance, weight gain, bradycardia, cognitive slowing.
pharmacology: Levothyroxine (T4): peripheral conversion to T3 (active form); long half-life (7 days) allows once-daily dosing. T3 (liothyronine): direct active form; faster onset, shorter duration.
dispensing_guidance: Levothyroxine: take on empty stomach, 30-60 min before food (absorption reduced by food, calcium, iron). Dose titrated to TSH (goal 0.5-2.5 mIU/L typically). Takes 6-8 weeks to reach steady-state. Many drug interactions (iron, calcium, PPIs ↓ absorption). Monitor symptoms + labs.

title: ANTIBACTERIAL DRUGS
pathophysiology: Bacterial infections: pathogenic bacteria cause inflammation via toxins, LPS, or direct invasion. Severity ranges from minor (UTI, otitis) to life-threatening (sepsis, meningitis, endocarditis).
pharmacology: Beta-lactams (amoxicillin, cephalexin, ceftriaxone): inhibit transpeptidase → cell wall synthesis inhibition (bactericidal). Macrolides (erythromycin, azithromycin): 30S ribosome inhibition → protein synthesis (bacteriostatic). Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin): DNA gyrase/topoisomerase IV inhibition → DNA replication failure (bactericidal). Tetracyclines (doxycycline, tetracycline): 30S inhibition (bacteriostatic). Aminoglycosides (gentamicin): 30S inhibition → misreading (bactericidal at high concentrations).
dispensing_guidance: Beta-lactams: full course 7-14 days (resistance development + relapse if shortened). Monitor for allergic reactions (cross-reactivity with cephalosporins ~1%). Macrolides: GI upset (take with food); QT prolongation risk (monitor). Fluoroquinolones: tendon rupture risk (elderly, corticosteroid users); avoid in children (cartilage concern). Tetracyclines: photosensitivity (sunscreen); esophageal ulcers if swallowed dry. Aminoglycosides: dose by weight/renal function; nephrotoxicity + ototoxicity monitoring.

title: ANTIMYCOTIC DRUGS
pathophysiology: Systemic fungal infections (candemia, aspergillosis, cryptococcosis): opportunistic in neutropenia, HIV, post-transplant states.
pharmacology: Azoles (fluconazole, voriconazole): as D01 (systemic penetration better in azoles than topical). Polyenes (amphotericin B): lipid formulations (liposomal ampho B) reduce nephrotoxicity vs. conventional. Echinocandins (caspofungin): inhibit β-1,3-glucan synthesis → unique fungal cell wall target.
dispensing_guidance: Amphotericin B: IV infusion (nephrotoxicity risk; baseline + periodic creatinine, K+, Mg2+ monitoring). Premedication with acetaminophen + diphenhydramine reduces infusion reactions. Echinocandins: preferred empiric therapy in many candidiasis cases (less drug interactions). Fluconazole: oral or IV; excellent tissue penetration (meningitis coverage). Monitor LFTs.

title: ANTIMYCOBACTERIALS
pathophysiology: Viral infections: intracellular obligate parasites; range from acute (influenza, COVID) to chronic (HIV, HBV, HCV).
pharmacology: Nucleoside analogs (acyclovir, AZT): incorporate into viral DNA → chain termination. Protease inhibitors (ritonavir, lopinavir): HIV protease inhibition → immature virion formation. NNRTI (efavirenz, rilpivirine): reverse transcriptase allosteric inhibition. Integrase inhibitors (dolutegravir): HIV integration into host genome blockade. Direct-acting antivirals for HCV (sofosbuvir, daclatasvir): NS5B/NS5A protein targeting.
dispensing_guidance: Antiretrovirals: absolute adherence required (>95%) for resistance prevention. Monitor CD4, viral load. Drug interactions (ritonavir is potent CYP3A4 inhibitor—many interactions). Side effects vary (GI with protease inhibitors, rash with NNRTIs). Acyclovir: IV for severe infections; high-dose prophylaxis in immunocompromised.

title: PLANT ALKALOIDS AND OTHER NATURAL PLANTS
ANTIINFLAMMATORY AND ANTIRHEUMATIC DRUGS
pathophysiology: Rheumatoid arthritis: Th1/Th17 cells infiltrate synovium → TNF-α, IL-6 production → osteoclast activation (bone erosion) + synovitis (pain, swelling). Osteoarthritis: cartilage degradation via chondrocyte senescence + mechanical stress.
pharmacology: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, indomethacin, diclofenac): COX-1/2 inhibition → ↓ prostaglandins (pain, inflammation). DMARDs (methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, sulfasalazine): slow-acting; various mechanisms (folate antagonism, autophagy, TNF modulation). Biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 antagonists): targeted immunosuppression. Corticosteroids: as H02 (intra-articular injections for localized inflammation).
dispensing_guidance: NSAIDs: GI upset (use gastroprotection—PPI or misoprostol); cardiovascular risk (especially COX-2 selective); renal dysfunction with chronic use. Monitor BP, renal function. Avoid in PUD history. Methotrexate: monitor CBC, LFTs; teratogenic (pregnancy CI); folate supplementation. Biologics: infection risk (TB screening mandatory); expensive; reserve for inadequate NSAID/DMARD response.

title: TOPICAL PRODUCTS FOR JOINT AND MUSCULAR PAIN
pathophysiology: Local musculoskeletal pain from trauma, overuse, inflammation.
pharmacology: Topical NSAIDs (diclofenac, ibuprofen): local penetration → COX inhibition (reduced systemic side effects vs. oral). Capsaicin: TRPV1 desensitization → reduced pain transmission. Menthol: local cooling + TRPM8 activation → analgesia.
dispensing_guidance: Topical NSAIDs: adjunctive (less systemic exposure but still some absorption—monitor in renal/CV disease). Apply QID; wash hands after (unless treating hands). Capsaicin: 3-4 times daily; initial burning (transient). Warn about contamination on mucous membranes (eye exposure = intense burning).

title: MUSCLE RELAXANTS
pathophysiology: Muscle spasm from injury, inflammation, or neurological disease (MS, CP) causes pain, limitation of motion.
pharmacology: Spinal muscle relaxants (baclofen, tizanidine): GABA-B agonism (baclofen) or α2 agonism (tizanidine) → inhibition of motor neurons + spinal reflexes. Skeletal muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine, methocarbamol): direct smooth muscle effect (mechanism not fully defined).
dispensing_guidance: Baclofen: dosing by titration (start 5 mg TID → 20 mg TID); renal impairment requires dose reduction. Risk of spasticity rebound if abruptly discontinued (taper essential). Tizanidine: monitor BP, LFTs (hepatotoxicity rare). Cyclobenzaprine: sedating (take at night); short half-life. Not for prolonged spasticity (limited efficacy).

title: ANTIGOUT PREPARATIONS
pathophysiology: Gout: monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints → NLRP3 inflammasome activation → IL-1β release → intense inflammation.
pharmacology: Acute attack: NSAIDs/colchicine: COX inhibition or microtubule disruption → reduced neutrophil infiltration. Urate-lowering: allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibition) or febuxostat (xanthine oxidase inhibitor, selective). Uricosuric agents (probenecid): ↑ renal urate excretion.
dispensing_guidance: Colchicine: GI toxicity (cramping, diarrhea) dose-limiting; monitor renal function (accumulation if CrCl <30). Start prophylaxis 1-2 weeks before urate-lowering (prevent flare). Allopurinol: 1st-line (cheap); titrate by serum urate (goal <6 mg/dL). Febuxostat: alternative if allopurinol contraindicated. Probenecid: adequate fluid intake (prevent stone formation).

title: GENERAL ANESTHETICS
pathophysiology: Surgical trauma triggers nociceptor activation → pain. Consciousness requires thalamic reticular nucleus activity.
pharmacology: Local anesthetics (lidocaine, bupivacaine): Na+ channel blockade → conduction blockade. Topical anesthetics (procaine): surface application. General anesthetics (propofol, thiopental): GABA-A enhancement (CNS depression). Neuromuscular blocking agents (succinylcholine, atracurium): nicotinic acetylcholine receptor antagonism → paralysis.
dispensing_guidance: Local anesthetics: aspirate before injection (prevent IV injection → cardiotoxicity). Maximum doses by weight. Bupivacaine: cardiotoxic if injected IV (long-acting). Propofol: IV only; hypotension, respiratory depression. Succinylcholine: depolarizing; K+ release (contraindicated in burns, crush injury, renal failure—hyperkalemia risk). Atracurium: non-depolarizing; slower onset but safer.

title: OTHER ANALGESICS AND ANTIPYRETICS
pathophysiology: Pain: nociceptor activation (mechanical, thermal, chemical stimuli) → dorsal horn synapse → ascending tracts → brain cortex.
pharmacology: NSAIDs: as M01. Opioids (morphine, codeine, tramadol): μ-opioid receptor activation → ↓ pain signal transmission + ↑ descending inhibition. Paracetamol (acetaminophen): COX inhibition + CB1 activation → analgesia. Local anesthetics: as N01.
dispensing_guidance: NSAIDs: as M01 (GI, CV, renal risks). Opioids: respiratory depression, addiction risk (titrate cautiously). Monitor respiratory rate, sedation. Tolerance develops → escalating doses. Naloxone reversal if overdose. Constipation universal (prophylaxis with laxatives). Paracetamol: max 4 g/day (hepatotoxicity); caution in liver disease. Tramadol: SNRI + opioid effect; serotonin syndrome risk (avoid with SSRIs).

title: MIGRAINE MEDICATION
pathophysiology: Epilepsy: recurrent seizures from neuronal hyperexcitability (Na+ channel dysfunction, GABA deficiency, glutamate excess).
pharmacology: Sodium channel blockers (phenytoin, carbamazepine, lamotrigine): ↓ neuronal firing frequency. GABA enhancers (valproic acid, benzodiazepines): GABA-A potentiation. Calcium channel blockers (ethosuximide): thalamic T-type blockade (absence seizures). Gabapentin: unknown (GABA analog; not GABA agonist). Levetiracetam: binds SV2A → synaptic vesicle modulation.
dispensing_guidance: Phenytoin: narrow therapeutic window (10-20 mcg/mL); non-linear kinetics. Monitor levels, signs of toxicity (ataxia, nystagmus). Drug interactions (CYP450 inducer). Carbamazepine: aplastic anemia risk (rare; CBC baseline + periodic monitoring). Valproic acid: hepatotoxicity (especially <2 years age); monitor LFTs. Lamotrigine: skin rash (SJS risk if titrated too fast—slow titration mandatory). Levetiracetam: behavioral changes (mood, aggression; counsel patients). Never abruptly discontinue (seizure rebound).

title: ANTICHOLINERGIC AGENTS  FOR PARKINSON
pathophysiology: SNpc). Normal SNpc dopaminergic neurons project via nigrostriatal pathway to striatum (putamen/caudate) → dopamine release onto D1 receptors (direct pathway → excitation → movement facilitation) and D2 receptors (indirect pathway → inhibition → movement suppression). Net effect of DA: disinhibits thalamus → cortical motor activation. With >60-70% SNpc neuron loss + >80% striatal dopamine depletion → clinical PD: resting tremor (4-6 Hz, 'pill-rolling'), rigidity (lead-pipe/cogwheel), bradykinesia (slowness/poverty of movement), postural instability. Non-motor features: autonomic dysfunction (constipation, orthostatic hypotension, urinary urgency), sleep disturbance (REM sleep behaviour disorder — often precedes motor symptoms by years), hyposmia, depression, cognitive impairment → dementia. Alpha-synuclein spreads in Braak stages (gut → brain via vagus; olfactory bulb → limbic → neocortex). Secondary parkinsonism: drug-induced (dopamine antagonists — antipsychotics, metoclopramide), vascular, post-encephalitic, MSA, PSP, CBD.
pharmacology: Levodopa (L-DOPA): amino acid precursor to dopamine; crosses blood-brain barrier via large neutral amino acid transporter (LAT1) — dopamine itself does NOT cross BBB. In brain: aromatic amino acid decarboxylase (AADC) converts L-DOPA → dopamine in remaining nigrostriatal terminals and astrocytes. Peripherally: 95% converted to dopamine by AADC (nausea, vomiting, hypotension, arrhythmias). Carbidopa/benserazide: peripheral AADC inhibitors (do not cross BBB) → block peripheral L-DOPA conversion → ↑ brain L-DOPA availability + ↓ peripheral dopamine side effects → dose of L-DOPA reduced 75%. Levodopa/carbidopa (Sinemet, Madopar): gold standard pharmacotherapy. Long-term L-DOPA: dyskinesias (involuntary choreiform movements) from pulsatile stimulation of sensitized striatal DA receptors; motor fluctuations ('wearing off' — end-of-dose deterioration; 'on-off' phenomenon). Dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole, rotigotine patch, cabergoline, bromocriptine): directly stimulate D2/D3 receptors (pramipexole/ropinirole D3>D2; cabergoline D2). Less pulsatile stimulation vs L-DOPA → lower dyskinesia risk. Monotherapy in younger PD patients to delay L-DOPA. Less effective than L-DOPA. Non-ergot (pramipexole, ropinirole) preferred over ergot (cabergoline, bromocriptine) — no fibrosis risk. MAO-B inhibitors (selegiline, rasagiline, safinamide): selectively inhibit MAO-B (predominant form in striatum) → ↓ dopamine breakdown → ↑ striatal dopamine. Modest symptomatic + possible neuroprotective effect. COMT inhibitors (entacapone, tolcapone): inhibit catechol-O-methyltransferase → ↓ peripheral L-DOPA degradation → prolonged L-DOPA half-life → smoother motor effect → ↓ 'wearing off'. Entacapone co-formulated with L-DOPA/carbidopa (Stalevo). Tolcapone: more potent but rare fatal hepatotoxicity → restricted use. Amantadine: weak NMDA antagonist → reduces dyskinesias; also weak dopaminergic. Anticholinergics (trihexyphenidyl, benztropine): reduce relative cholinergic excess (dopamine normally inhibits striatal cholinergic interneurons — loss of DA → cholinergic hyperactivity → tremor/rigidity). Most useful for tremor in young patients. Avoid in elderly (anticholinergic side effects, cognitive impairment). Apomorphine (SC): potent non-selective DA agonist; subcutaneous injection or pump; used for rescue from 'off' episodes or continuous infusion via pump in advanced PD.
dispensing_guidance: L-DOPA/carbidopa: initiate low (100/25 mg TID) and titrate every 1-2 weeks. Take 30-60 min before meals or 2 hours after (protein competes with L-DOPA for LAT1 transport → delayed or reduced absorption with protein-rich meals; protein redistribution diet in some patients). Nausea: common initially (domperidone — NOT metoclopramide/prochlorperazine/haloperidol as these cross BBB and worsen parkinsonism); takes 2-4 weeks to resolve. Motor fluctuations: options include increasing dose frequency, adding COMT inhibitor (entacapone) or MAO-B inhibitor, continuous duodenal infusion, or deep brain stimulation (DBS). Controlled-release L-DOPA: less predictable absorption — generally not preferred except overnight. NEVER abruptly stop L-DOPA (neuroleptic malignant syndrome-like syndrome — hyperthermia, rigidity, confusion). Dopamine agonists: IMPULSE CONTROL DISORDERS (ICDs) — pathological gambling, hypersexuality, binge eating, compulsive shopping in 10-17%; screen at every visit; dose reduction or switch if develops (resolve on stopping usually). Orthostatic hypotension (all DA agents, L-DOPA). Daytime somnolence, sleep attacks (sudden sleep onset — driving risk — must counsel patient and consider driving fitness; risk highest with dopamine agonists). Leg edema (pramipexole). Hallucinations and psychosis — dose reduction; if persisting, clozapine (12.5-25 mg at night — unique non-dopamine-blocking antipsychotic, safe in PD) or quetiapine (off-label). Selegiline: mild amphetamine metabolites (insomnia — avoid evening doses). Rasagiline: avoid tyramine-rich foods in high doses (weak MAOI properties — hypertensive crisis). Interactions: tramadol, SSRIs, SNRIs + MAO-B inhibitors → serotonin syndrome. Apomorphine: severe nausea/vomiting without domperidone pretreatment; rotate injection sites (subcutaneous nodules); QTc prolongation. DBS: refer advanced PD with good L-DOPA response but disabling motor fluctuations/dyskinesias. Neuroprotection: no confirmed neuroprotective treatment in clinical use.

title: ANTIPSYCHOTIC  DRUGS
pathophysiology: Psychotic disorders — schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder, psychotic depression, bipolar mania — share positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, thought disorder) from excessive dopaminergic transmission in mesolimbic pathway (VTA → nucleus accumbens); and negative symptoms (anhedonia, avolition, flat affect, social withdrawal) and cognitive dysfunction from dopaminergic hypofunction in mesocortical pathway (VTA → prefrontal cortex). Glutamate hypofunction (NMDA receptor dysfunction) also central — explains negative symptoms and cognitive features. Dopamine hypothesis: 1950s observation that chlorpromazine (D2 blocker) relieved psychosis; all effective antipsychotics block D2 receptors (minimum 60-65% D2 occupancy for antipsychotic effect; >80% → EPS). Serotonergic pathway (5-HT2A in prefrontal cortex) modulates dopamine release — 5-HT2A antagonism (atypicals) restores mesocortical dopamine → ↓ negative symptoms.
pharmacology: First-generation (typical) antipsychotics (FGAs): Haloperidol, chlorpromazine, fluphenazine, perphenazine, trifluoperazine, thioridazine. Potent D2 blockade (nigrostriatal → EPS; tuberoinfundibular → hyperprolactinaemia). Chlorpromazine: low-potency, more sedating, more anticholinergic. Haloperidol: high-potency, less sedating, more EPS. Second-generation (atypical) antipsychotics (SGAs): Risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole, clozapine, paliperidone, ziprasidone, amisulpride, lurasidone. D2 + 5-HT2A blockade (5-HT2A block in mesocortical pathway → DA release → ↓ negative symptoms; 5-HT2A block in nigrostriatal pathway → DA release → offsetting D2 block → ↓ EPS). Clozapine: uniquely effective for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (TRS); low D2 affinity/fast dissociation + broad receptor profile (H1, α1, M1, 5-HT2A); associated with agranulocytosis (requires mandatory haematological monitoring); weight gain + metabolic syndrome; seizures; myocarditis/cardiomyopathy; hypersalivation. Aripiprazole: D2/D3 partial agonist (functional antagonist at hyperdopaminergic sites, agonist at hypodopaminergic sites) — unique 'dopamine system stabilizer'; less weight gain, less prolactin elevation. Quetiapine: metabolized to norquetiapine (antidepressant properties — NE reuptake inhibition) → used for bipolar depression. Long-acting injectable (LAI/depot) formulations: paliperidone palmitate (monthly/3-monthly), risperidone microspheres (biweekly), haloperidol decanoate (monthly), aripiprazole lauroxil (monthly/6-weekly) → bypass first-pass, consistent plasma levels, better adherence in patients with insight/compliance issues.
dispensing_guidance: Acute psychosis: oral or IM antipsychotic + benzodiazepine (lorazepam IM) for agitation. Haloperidol 5-10 mg IM for acute control. Extrapyramidal side effects (EPS): Acute dystonia (within hours-days): sustained painful muscle spasm, oculogyric crisis, torticollis — treat with IM/IV anticholinergic (benztropine 1-2 mg or diphenhydramine 25-50 mg) immediately. Akathisia (within days-weeks): intense subjective restlessness, inability to sit still — β-blocker (propranolol 10-30 mg TID) or mirtazapine; reduce dose; switch to SGA. Parkinsonism (weeks): bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor from D2 blockade — reduce dose; add procyclidine/trihexyphenidyl; switch to atypical. Tardive dyskinesia (TD — months-years): repetitive involuntary orofacial/limb movements from D2 receptor upregulation (supersensitivity) — REDUCE/STOP offending drug; may be irreversible; valbenazine/deutetrabenazine (VMAT2 inhibitors) approved for treatment. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS): life-threatening idiosyncratic reaction — hyperthermia, lead-pipe rigidity, autonomic instability (BP, HR), altered consciousness, ↑ CK. STOP antipsychotic immediately; supportive ICU care; dantrolene, bromocriptine. Metabolic syndrome (SGAs — especially olanzapine, clozapine): weight gain, dyslipidaemia, impaired fasting glucose/T2DM, hypertension. Monitor: weight/BMI, waist circumference, fasting glucose, lipids, BP at baseline, 3 months, annually. Olanzapine: most weight gain of all SGAs (5-10 kg/year). QTc prolongation: all antipsychotics to varying degrees — ECG at baseline; especially sertindole, ziprasidone, pimozide — avoid with other QTc-prolonging agents. Prolactin elevation (haloperidol, risperidone, amisulpride ↑↑): galactorrhoea, amenorrhoea, sexual dysfunction, gynecomastia, ↓ bone density. Clozapine monitoring: MANDATORY weekly FBC for first 18 weeks → biweekly for next 26 weeks → monthly (absolute neutrophil count: suspend if ANC <2000, stop if <1500 and NEVER rechallenge with clozapine if ANC <1000). Clozapine dispensing: ONLY through restricted dispensing program (CPMS/CMS). Titrate slowly from 12.5 mg → max 300-900 mg/day. Sedation, orthostatic hypotension, hypersalivation, constipation (severe — ileus risk; prescribe laxatives), urinary incontinence, weight gain, seizures (>600 mg/day risk — add valproate prophylaxis). Myocarditis screening (troponin + CRP weeks 1-4). LAI antipsychotics: ensure adequate plasma levels from oral drug before first LAI injection (especially paliperidone palmitate — established overlap not needed). Administer by trained staff; gluteal or deltoid; observe after for 3 hours (injection-related reactions).

title: ANXIOLYTIC DRUGS
pathophysiology: Anxiety disorders (GAD, panic disorder, social anxiety, specific phobias, PTSD) involve: ↑ amygdala reactivity (fear circuitry hyperactivity), ↓ prefrontal cortical inhibition of amygdala, altered HPA axis (CRH hypersecretion → cortisol), and GABAergic/serotonergic dysfunction. GAD: chronic uncontrollable worry + physical symptoms (muscle tension, fatigue, insomnia, irritability, concentration difficulty). Panic disorder: episodic overwhelming fear with autonomic surge (palpitations, dyspnea, chest pain, dizziness, derealization, fear of dying/losing control). GABA (γ-aminobutyric acid) is the primary inhibitory CNS neurotransmitter: GABA-A receptor is a pentameric ligand-gated Cl⁻ channel → Cl⁻ influx → hyperpolarization → ↓ neuronal firing. Reduced GABAergic tone in amygdala → ↑ anxiety.
pharmacology: Benzodiazepines (diazepam, lorazepam, alprazolam, clonazepam, oxazepam, temazepam, midazolam): positive allosteric modulators of GABA-A at benzodiazepine-binding site (α-subunit adjacent to γ-subunit) → ↑ frequency of Cl⁻ channel opening (NOT amplitude — barbiturates ↑ duration) → hyperpolarization → ↓ amygdala/limbic hyperactivity → anxiolytic, sedative, muscle relaxant, anticonvulsant, amnesic. All BZDs are full agonists with similar mechanisms — differences are pharmacokinetic. Short-acting (lorazepam, oxazepam, temazepam): no active metabolites (safer in elderly/liver disease; less accumulation). Long-acting (diazepam, chlordiazepoxide): active metabolites (self-tapering, useful for BZD withdrawal). Azapirones (buspirone): 5-HT1A partial agonist in raphe nuclei → ↓ serotonergic transmission → anxiolytic (no sedation, no addiction, no respiratory depression, no GABAergic effects). Onset 2-4 weeks. Effective for GAD maintenance. Hydroxyzine: H1 antihistamine + anticholinergic → sedating anxiolytic; no addiction potential; used for acute anxiety and pruritus. Pregabalin: α2δ VGCC subunit binding → ↓ glutamate release (anxiolytic mechanism). Effective for GAD, social anxiety, PTSD. SSRIs/SNRIs: first-line for chronic anxiety disorders (see N06A) — onset 4-6 weeks.
dispensing_guidance: Benzodiazepines: use for SHORT DURATIONS only (2-4 weeks maximum) due to: tolerance (rapidly develops to sedative/anxiolytic effects — does not develop to anticonvulsant/amnesic), physical dependence (GABA-A receptor downregulation — withdrawal syndrome on cessation), cognitive impairment (memory, attention, reaction time — driving risk), falls in elderly (3x ↑ hip fracture risk), abuse potential. Prescribe minimum effective dose, lowest potency, for defined short course. Withdrawal syndrome: rebound anxiety, insomnia, tremor, sweating, tachycardia, seizures (can be life-threatening with long-acting, high-dose BZD dependence — similar to alcohol withdrawal). Taper gradually over weeks-months (equivalent diazepam dose conversion → diazepam taper 10% reduction per week maximum). Lorazepam: no active metabolites — preferred in liver disease, elderly. Absorbed IM (diazepam not reliably absorbed IM — use IV). Buspirone: explain 2-4 week onset (patients expect immediate effect like BZDs — adherence problem). Prior BZD users respond less well. Pregabalin: titrate from 25-75 mg to 150-450 mg/day for anxiety. Abuse/misuse potential (especially in substance use disorder patients). Monitor weight (↑↑), dizziness, somnolence. Antidepressants (SSRIs/SNRIs) should be first-line for all chronic anxiety disorders with BZDs reserved only for adjunctive short-term use during initiation of SSRI.

title: HYPNOTICS AND SEDATIVES DRUGS
pathophysiology: Insomnia: impaired sleep initiation, maintenance, or non-restorative sleep causing daytime impairment. Pathophysiology: hyperarousal model — ↑ activity of ascending arousal systems (noradrenergic locus coeruleus, serotonergic raphe, histaminergic tuberomammillary nucleus, orexinergic lateral hypothalamus, cholinergic basal forebrain) fails to be inhibited by sleep-promoting VLPO (ventrolateral preoptic nucleus — GABAergic/galaninergic). Chronic insomnia: conditioned hyperarousal (Spielman 3-P model: predisposing × precipitating × perpetuating factors). Sleep architecture: NREM (N1-light, N2-spindles/K-complexes, N3-slow wave) and REM cycling every 90 min; deep sleep predominantly early night, REM increases toward morning.
pharmacology: Z-drugs (zopiclone, zolpidem, zaleplon): non-benzodiazepine GABA-A positive allosteric modulators (similar to BZDs but more selective for α1-subunit containing GABA-A receptors — sedation, amnesia; less anxiolysis, muscle relaxation than classic BZDs). Short-acting (zaleplon t½ 1 hr, zolpidem t½ 2 hr, zopiclone t½ 5 hr). Melatonin: endogenous hormone secreted by pineal gland (MT1/MT2 receptor agonism) in response to darkness (circadian phase marker) → ↓ alertness + synchronization of circadian rhythm. Modest hypnotic effect; most useful for jet lag and circadian rhythm disorders. Ramelteon: MT1/MT2 agonist (more potent than melatonin). Low-dose doxepin: H1 antagonism at 3-6 mg → sleep maintenance insomnia (distinct from antidepressant doses). Orexin receptor antagonists (suvorexant): blocks OX1R and OX2R → ↓ arousal signal from lateral hypothalamus → promotes sleep without GABAergic CNS depression.
dispensing_guidance: First-line for chronic insomnia: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) — superior to pharmacotherapy for long-term outcomes; includes stimulus control, sleep restriction, relaxation, cognitive restructuring, sleep hygiene. Pharmacotherapy adjunctive or short-term only. Z-drugs: similar risks to benzodiazepines (tolerance, dependence, cognitive impairment, parasomnia — sleepwalking/complex sleep-related behaviours with zolpidem; avoid with alcohol). Prescribe for 2-4 weeks. Elderly: ↑ risk falls, cognitive impairment, morning sedation (half-life prolonged in elderly) — use lowest dose; zopiclone 3.75 mg (half-tablet) in elderly (vs. 7.5 mg adults). Zolpidem gender difference: women clear more slowly → FDA lowered dose recommendation for women (5 mg vs 10 mg). Melatonin: 2-5 mg 30-60 min before bedtime; most useful for circadian phase disorders (shift work, jet lag). Low dependence risk. Antihistamines (diphenhydramine, promethazine): rapid tolerance (days) → ineffective for chronic insomnia; next-day sedation; anticholinergic (elderly — avoid per Beers criteria). Address comorbidities contributing to insomnia (pain, depression, anxiety, OSA, restless legs — treat RLS with dopamine agonists, iron supplementation).

title: ANTIDEPRESSANTS DRUGS
pathophysiology: Major depressive disorder (MDD): monoamine hypothesis — deficiency of serotonin (5-HT), norepinephrine (NE), and dopamine (DA) in limbic system and prefrontal cortex. More contemporary: neuroplasticity hypothesis — ↓ BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor) → hippocampal atrophy and synaptic loss in PFC. HPA axis dysregulation (↑ cortisol → glucocorticoid receptor downregulation in hippocampus → ↓ negative feedback → chronically elevated cortisol → hippocampal damage). Inflammatory hypothesis — ↑ IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1 activate IDO (indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase) → tryptophan → kynurenine (not serotonin) → ↓ 5-HT + neurotoxic kynurenines. Treatment with antidepressants: latency of 4-6 weeks before clinical benefit (adaptation of receptor sensitivity, ↑ BDNF, neurogenesis required). All effective antidepressants ultimately ↑ monoaminergic transmission via different mechanisms. Dysthymia: chronic lower-grade depression ≥2 years. Bipolar depression: depressive episodes in bipolar disorder (antidepressants may trigger mania — use with mood stabilizer).
pharmacology: SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline, paroxetine, citalopram, escitalopram, fluvoxamine): inhibit SERT (serotonin transporter) → ↑ synaptic 5-HT → initial autoreceptor desensitization (weeks) → sustained 5-HT effect. Escitalopram/citalopram: most serotonin-selective (fewest drug interactions). Fluoxetine: longest half-life (t½ 1-4 days; active metabolite norfluoxetine t½ 7-15 days) → less discontinuation syndrome; potential for prolonged drug interactions. Paroxetine: most anticholinergic SSRI; shortest half-life → worst discontinuation syndrome; most CYP2D6 inhibition. SNRIs (venlafaxine, desvenlafaxine, duloxetine, levomilnacipran): inhibit SERT + NET (norepinephrine transporter) → ↑ 5-HT + NE. Duloxetine: balanced dual reuptake inhibition; also effective for neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, stress urinary incontinence. Venlafaxine: primarily serotonergic at low doses; NE effect at ≥150 mg. TCAs (amitriptyline, clomipramine, nortriptyline, imipramine, desipramine): block SERT, NET, H1, M1 (anticholinergic), α1 (hypotension), Na⁺ channels (cardiac — QRS widening), + multiple others → broad receptor profile → sedation + weight gain + cardiac toxicity. Amitriptyline: highest anticholinergic; most sedating. Nortriptyline: active metabolite of amitriptyline; better tolerated; least hypotensive. MAOIs (phenelzine, tranylcypromine, moclobemide): irreversible MAO-A/B inhibition (phenelzine, tranylcypromine) → ↑ 5-HT, NE, DA; dietary tyramine (normally metabolized by intestinal/hepatic MAO) bypasses MAO → systemic absorption → ↑ NE → hypertensive crisis (palpitations, headache, flushing, stroke risk). Moclobemide: reversible MAO-A inhibitor (RIMA) — far safer tyramine interaction profile (reversible displacement by high tyramine). Mirtazapine: α2 autoreceptor antagonist → ↑ 5-HT and NE release; 5-HT2 and 5-HT3 antagonism (↓ nausea, ↓ insomnia); H1 blockade (sedation, weight gain). Effective as augmentation or monotherapy. Bupropion: NE and DA reuptake inhibitor (NDRI); no serotonergic → no sexual dysfunction; stimulant-like; also for smoking cessation. Seizure risk dose-dependent (CI in eating disorders, seizure history). Trazodone: SARI (serotonin antagonist and reuptake inhibitor); mainly 5-HT2A antagonism → insomnia (low dose 50-100 mg). Agomelatine: MT1/MT2 agonist + 5-HT2C antagonist → normalises circadian rhythmicity + ↑ NE/DA in PFC. Vortioxetine: SERT inhibitor + 5-HT1A agonist + 5-HT3/7 antagonist → broad serotonin modulation + cognitive benefits.
dispensing_guidance: SSRIs: start low and titrate gradually (activating effects [anxiety, agitation, insomnia] in first 2 weeks — counsel on transience). Explain 4-6 week full effect onset — critical for adherence (most patients stop prematurely). Sexual dysfunction (↓ libido, anorgasmia, delayed ejaculation) common and persistent (40-60%; consider dose reduction, drug holiday [not for fluoxetine], switching to bupropion/agomelatine/mirtazapine, or adding buspirone). Hyponatraemia (SIADH) — especially in elderly (check Na⁺ at 2-4 weeks in elderly). GI side effects: nausea (transient — take with food). Weight change: fluoxetine/bupropion → weight neutral/↓; paroxetine/mirtazapine → weight ↑. Discontinuation syndrome (all SSRIs, especially paroxetine/venlafaxine): dizziness, electric-shock sensations ('brain zaps'), nausea, anxiety, flu-like symptoms on abrupt cessation or missed doses — taper slowly over weeks-months, never abrupt stop. Switching antidepressants: washout period especially from MAOIs (2 weeks after irreversible MAOI before SSRI; 5 weeks after fluoxetine before MAOI) to avoid serotonin syndrome. Serotonin syndrome: excessive serotonergic stimulation (SSRIs + tramadol/fentanyl/linezolid/triptans/St John's Wort/methamphetamine) → triad of cognitive changes (agitation, confusion), autonomic instability (fever, tachycardia, diaphoresis), and neuromuscular abnormalities (clonus [most specific], hyperreflexia, tremor, myoclonus). Treatment: stop serotonergic drugs, cyproheptadine (5-HT2A antagonist), supportive care. Duration of treatment: first episode uncomplicated MDD — minimum 6-12 months after remission before tapering (relapse prevention). Two+ episodes or high-risk features: 2 years to indefinite. Recurrent/chronic: long-term. TCAs: anticholinergic side effects (dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, blurred vision, confusion); orthostatic hypotension; cardiac — baseline ECG (QRS widening; QTc prolongation — arrhythmia risk especially in OD). Cardiotoxic in overdose (risk even at moderate excess — prescribe limited quantities). Avoid in elderly if possible (Beers criteria). Useful: amitriptyline for neuropathic pain (10-75 mg), migraine prophylaxis, insomnia at low doses. MAOIs: tyramine-restricted diet (aged cheeses, cured meats, pickled/fermented foods, red wine, soy sauce, Marmite, broad beans, overripe bananas) during use and 2 weeks after. Multiple drug interactions. Phenelzine/tranylcypromine: last resort for treatment-resistant/atypical depression (hypersomnia, hyperphagia, mood reactivity, rejection sensitivity). Mirtazapine: most sedating at 15 mg (H1 blockade dominant); paradoxically less sedating at higher doses (NE effect ↑). Weight gain substantial — use to advantage in depressed anorexic/insomniac patients. Bupropion: available as sustained release (300-450 mg/day). Lowers seizure threshold — contraindicated: bulimia/anorexia nervosa (electrolyte disturbance + seizure risk), history of seizures, abrupt alcohol withdrawal. Agomelatine: LFT monitoring (hepatotoxic — ALAT >3x ULN in ~1%; baseline LFTs + at 6, 12 weeks). No antidepressant shows superiority in efficacy — selection based on side-effect profile, patient comorbidities, prior response, and drug interactions.

title: AGENTS AGAINST AMOEBIASIS AND OTHER PROTOZOAL DISEASES
pathophysiology: Entamoeba histolytica: ingestion of infective cysts (feco-oral route, contaminated food/water) → trophozoites in colon → colonic invasion (penetrate mucosa → 'flask-shaped' ulcers → bloody/mucoid diarrhoea, colitis) → hepatic seeding via portal blood → amoebic liver abscess (single large right lobe abscess; fever + RUQ pain + no intestinal symptoms in 50%). Giardia lamblia: small intestinal trophozoites → malabsorption syndrome (secretory diarrhoea, bloating, flatulence, pale fatty stools), particularly in travellers, children, and immunocompromised. Cryptosporidium: intracellular intestinal pathogen → watery diarrhoea (self-limiting in immunocompetent; life-threatening in HIV/AIDS).
pharmacology: Metronidazole (as A07): 5-nitroimidazole → activated by parasitic ferredoxin-like proteins → cytotoxic radical → DNA damage → trophozoite death. Drug of choice for amoebiasis + giardiasis + trichomonas. Tinidazole: similar mechanism; longer half-life → 1-3 day courses (better compliance). Nitazoxanide: inhibits pyruvate:ferredoxin oxidoreductase (PFOR) pathway in anaerobes and protozoa; broader antiprotozoal spectrum (Cryptosporidium — rare agent effective for this).
dispensing_guidance: Intestinal amoebiasis: metronidazole 800 mg TID x 5-10 days (kills invasive trophozoites) FOLLOWED by luminal agent (diloxanide furoate 500 mg TID x 10 days or paromomycin — kills luminal cysts/trophozoites to prevent relapse). Amoebic liver abscess: metronidazole 750 mg TID x 10 days + aspiration if abscess >10 cm or no clinical response at 3-5 days. Giardiasis: metronidazole 400 mg TID x 5-7 days OR tinidazole 2 g single dose. Alcohol avoidance during and 48 hrs after metronidazole (disulfiram-like reaction). Check stool for ova and parasites × 3 before treatment (false negatives common). Household members/contacts should be screened and treated simultaneously. Waterborne disease: advise boiling water or use of WHO-approved filter.

title: ANTIMALARIALS
pathophysiology: Malaria: Plasmodium species (falciparum — most lethal, SE Asia, Africa; vivax — worldwide; malariae; ovale; knowlesi — zoonotic) transmitted by female Anopheles mosquito. Life cycle: sporozoites injected → hepatic stage (exoerythrocytic replication — silent; P. vivax/ovale form hypnozoites — dormant forms causing relapse weeks-years later) → merozoites released → red blood cell invasion → asexual erythrocytic cycle (trophozoite → schizont → merozoite release: 48-72 hr cyclical fever) + sexual gametocytes (infect mosquito → sexual reproduction → new sporozoites). P. falciparum pathogenesis: infected RBC cytoadherence to microvascular endothelium (PfEMP1 protein-ICAM-1/CD36/chondroitin sulfate A receptor) → microvascular obstruction → organ sequestration → cerebral malaria (coma, seizures), acute lung injury, AKI, severe anaemia, hypoglycaemia. Parasite digests haemoglobin in food vacuole → haem (toxic) → polymerised to haemozoin (inert). Artemisinin-Resistant P. falciparum emerging in Greater Mekong Subregion → treatment failure risk.
pharmacology: Chloroquine: lysosomotropic → concentrates in food vacuole → inhibits haem polymerisation → toxic haem accumulates → parasite lysis. Resistance (P. falciparum chloroquine resistance transporter CRT mutation) widespread. Still effective for P. vivax in many regions. Artemisinin and derivatives (artesunate, artemether, dihydroartemisinin — ACTs): endoperoxide bridge activated by haem Fe²⁺ → alkylating free radical → oxidative damage to parasite proteins + haem interaction → rapid parasite clearance. Artesunate IV: drug of choice for severe P. falciparum. ACTs (artemether-lumefantrine [Coartem/Riamet], artesunate-amodiaquine, artesunate-mefloquine): artemisinin clears rapidly (short t½ 1-2 hrs) + partner drug (longer t½) eliminates residual parasites → combination prevents resistance. Quinine + doxycycline: inhibit haem polymerisation (quinine) + protein synthesis (doxycycline); IV quinine in severe malaria if artesunate unavailable. Primaquine: 8-aminoquinoline; kills P. vivax/ovale hepatic hypnozoites (only drug class that clears liver stage — radical cure); also kills gametocytes (↓ transmission). Pyrimethamine + sulphadoxine (SP/Fansidar): DHFR + DHPS inhibition (folate antagonism) → used for intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) + children (IPTc) in Africa. Resistance widespread for treatment. Mefloquine: haem polymerisation inhibition; long t½ (2-4 weeks); chemoprophylaxis. CNS adverse effects (nightmares, anxiety, psychosis). Atovaquone-proguanil (Malarone): electron transport chain disruption (atovaquone) + proguanil synergy (DHFR inhibition); prophylaxis + treatment; well-tolerated; expensive.
dispensing_guidance: Uncomplicated P. falciparum (first-line per Rwanda/WHO guidelines): Artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem) 6-dose regimen: 4 tabs at diagnosis, 4 tabs 8 hrs later, then 4 tabs BID x 2 days. Take with fatty meal (↑ lumefantrine absorption ↑ 16x with fat). Complete full course even if asymptomatic. Severe P. falciparum: IV artesunate 2.4 mg/kg at 0, 12, 24 hrs → then daily until oral tolerated → switch to oral ACT. Quinine IV (10 mg/kg q8h) if artesunate unavailable. Intensive monitoring: glucose q1-2 hrs (quinine → hyperinsulinaemia → hypoglycaemia), ECG (quinine → QTc prolongation), fluid balance (over-hydration causes pulmonary oedema in severe malaria — restrict to maintenance), haematocrit, consciousness. Exchange transfusion no longer recommended. P. vivax/ovale radical cure: chloroquine 25 mg/kg over 3 days + primaquine 15 mg OD x 14 days (radical cure). Primaquine: check G6PD status before prescribing (primaquine → oxidant haemolysis → severe haemolytic anaemia in G6PD-deficient patients — common in Africa/Asia/Mediterranean, up to 30% males). If G6PD deficient: 45 mg primaquine once weekly x 8 weeks (safer regimen) or withhold. Pregnancy: chloroquine safe; artemisinin-based ACTs used from 2nd trimester; primaquine contraindicated in pregnancy (G6PD uncertain in fetus). IPTp: SP 3 doses (at 16, 28, 36 weeks) for pregnant women in endemic areas. Malaria prophylaxis for travellers (outbound): atovaquone-proguanil (most tolerable, no G6PD concern), doxycycline, or mefloquine depending on destination, duration, tolerability. ALWAYS check updated travel medicine guidelines (CDC, WHO, TMVC) for resistance patterns by specific region. Chemoprophylaxis must start before travel, continue during, and after return (product-specific timing).

title: ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCLUDING SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS
pathophysiology: Ectoparasites infest the skin surface. Scabies: Sarcoptes scabiei var. hominis mite burrows into stratum corneum → lays eggs → intensely pruritic sensitisation reaction (IgE + cell-mediated). Highly contagious (close skin-to-skin contact). Norwegian (crusted) scabies: hyperinfection in immunocompromised → thousands of mites → scales, minimal itch (blunted immune response). Pediculosis (lice): Pediculus humanus capitis (head lice), P. humanus corporis (body lice — vector for typhus, relapsing fever, trench fever), Phthirus pubis (pubic/crab lice). Feed on blood → local reaction, secondary infection. Environmental insecticide resistance common (permethrin/pyrethroid resistance in some populations).
pharmacology: Permethrin 5% cream: synthetic pyrethroid → binds Na⁺ channels in arthropod nerve membranes → sustained depolarisation → paralysis → death. Scabies: applied from neck down (head in infants/elderly/immunocompromised) x 8-12 hrs → wash off. Malathion 0.5%: organophosphate → acetylcholinesterase inhibition in insects → ACh accumulation → cholinergic toxidrome → death. Aqueous (alcohol-free) formulations for children/eczema. Ivermectin (oral): as P02 — also kills scabies mites (licensed for Norwegian scabies and permethrin-resistant scabies). Benzyl benzoate 25% lotion: alternative for scabies; cheaper but more irritating.
dispensing_guidance: Scabies: permethrin 5% cream from neck to toes (pay special attention to finger webs, wrists, genitalia, umbilicus); wash off after 8-12 hrs; repeat in 7 days (kills hatched larvae from eggs that survived 1st treatment — eggs permethrin-resistant). ALL household contacts and sexual partners treated simultaneously (to prevent reinfection — critical). Treat clothing/bedding (60°C wash or seal in bag 72 hrs). Post-scabetic itch may persist 2-6 weeks after successful treatment (delayed hypersensitivity reaction) — oral antihistamines + hydrocortisone 1% cream; do NOT re-treat unnecessarily (inappropriate repeat treatment does not help). Norwegian (crusted) scabies: systemic ivermectin 200 mcg/kg + topical permethrin; ISOLATE patient (highly contagious); treat all contacts regardless of symptoms. Head lice: permethrin 1% lotion 10-min application, repeat in 7-10 days; OR malathion 0.5% aqueous 8-12 hr application. 'Bug-busting': wet combing with fine-tooth comb every 3-4 days x 2 weeks (alternative — no insecticides). Nit (egg) comb essential regardless of treatment (dead nits remain until physically removed). Treat all household members with live lice simultaneously. Body lice: treat clothing/bedding (lice live in seams, not on skin); personal hygiene; insecticidal treatment of skin if needed.

title: . COUGH SUPPRESSANTS EXCLUDING COMBINATIONS WITH EXPECTORENTS
ANTIHISTAMINES FOR SYSTEMIC USE
pathophysiology: Allergic rhinitis, urticaria, angioedema, atopic eczema pruritus: as N05B/D04/R01 above. Mast cell and basophil IgE crosslinking → degranulation → histamine release from cytoplasmic granules. Histamine acts on: H1 receptors (smooth muscle contraction, ↑ vascular permeability, pruritus, pain, bronchoconstriction, CNS arousal via histaminergic tuberomammillary neurons), H2 receptors (gastric acid secretion, cardiac effects), H3 receptors (presynaptic autoreceptors — ↓ histamine synthesis/release), H4 receptors (immune cell chemotaxis, pruritus). Urticaria: IgE-mediated mast cell activation in dermis → localised oedema (wheal and flare). Angioedema: deeper submucosal/subcutaneous oedema (potential airway compromise if laryngeal). Anaphylaxis: systemic mast cell/basophil activation → cardiovascular collapse (↓ SVR, ↑ vascular permeability → distributive shock), bronchospasm, angioedema.
pharmacology: H1 antihistamines: competitive, reversible antagonists at H1 receptors. First-generation (sedating) H1-antihistamines (chlorpheniramine, diphenhydramine, promethazine, hydroxyzine, cyproheptadine): lipophilic → cross BBB → sedation (H1 antagonism in histaminergic arousal pathways) + anticholinergic (M1 blockade → dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision, confusion) effects. Significant CNS depression. Promethazine: also D2 and 5-HT2 antagonism → antiemetic. Second-generation (non-sedating) H1-antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine, desloratadine, levocetirizine, bilastine, rupatadine): lipophobic → P-gp efflux at BBB → minimal CNS penetration → minimal sedation. Longer duration (once-daily). Cetirizine: some anti-eosinophil activity (mild immunomodulatory effect); minor sedation in some individuals. Fexofenadine: P-glycoprotein substrate → virtually no CNS effects (safest for driving). Rupatadine: also PAF (platelet-activating factor) antagonism.
dispensing_guidance: Second-generation antihistamines: preferred for chronic conditions (AR, chronic urticaria) due to better safety/tolerability profile. Once daily (loratadine, fexofenadine, desloratadine, bilastine) for better adherence. Fexofenadine: avoid grapefruit juice and fruit juices (↓ OATP-mediated absorption). Cetirizine: may cause drowsiness in some patients despite being 2nd generation — caution with driving until individual response known. Loratadine: virtually no sedation; extensively CYP-metabolised. Bilastine: no food within 1 hr of dose (impairs absorption). First-generation: sedation used therapeutically (short-term insomnia, procedural sedation). AVOID in: elderly (Beers criteria: falls, delirium, urinary retention, confusion), glaucoma (anticholinergic → acute closure crisis), BPH (urinary retention), myasthenia gravis. Do NOT drive/operate machinery. Hydroxyzine: sedating, anxiolytic and antipruritic; useful in acute urticaria/eczema pruritus (25-50 mg TID-QID). Antihistamines for acute allergic reactions/urticaria: SECOND-LINE to epinephrine in anaphylaxis (antihistamines do NOT prevent/reverse cardiovascular collapse or bronchospasm — epinephrine 0.5 mg IM thigh is life-saving first-line treatment). Cetirizine/fexofenadine for non-life-threatening reactions. Chronic spontaneous urticaria: regular daily 2nd-gen antihistamine (not PRN) up to 4x standard dose before considering additional therapy (ciclosporin, omalizumab). Avoid alcohol with all antihistamines (additive CNS depression).

title: OTHER OPHTHALMOLOGICAL PREPARATIONS
pathophysiology: Otitis externa (swimmer's ear): disrupted cerumen (earwax) protective barrier + humid environment → bacterial (Pseudomonas aeruginosa most common, S. aureus) or fungal (Aspergillus niger, Candida) colonization of external ear canal → oedema, inflammation, pain on tragus pressure, discharge. Risk factors: swimming, excessive ear cleaning/instrumentation, hearing aids, narrow canals, eczema. Otitis media with effusion (glue ear): Eustachian tube dysfunction → negative middle ear pressure → transudate accumulation → hearing loss (conductive) without acute infection. Acute otitis media (AOM): viral URTI → Eustachian tube inflammation/obstruction → bacterial superinfection (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis) in middle ear cleft → otalgia, fever, bulging TM (tympanic membrane) → possible TM perforation → purulent discharge.
pharmacology: Topical antibiotics (ofloxacin, ciprofloxacin, framycetin, neomycin, gentamicin): direct antimicrobial in ear canal/middle ear. Fluoroquinolones (ofloxacin, ciprofloxacin): most effective for OE; safe with perforated TM (non-ototoxic — unlike aminoglycosides). Aminoglycosides (neomycin, gentamicin): ototoxic if TM perforated or in middle ear — should NOT be used when TM integrity uncertain. Antifungal drops (clotrimazole 1%): for otomycosis. Topical steroids (fluocinolone, betamethasone, dexamethasone): reduce canal oedema and inflammation in otitis externa (particularly important when oedema prevents adequate antibiotic penetration — consider wick/aural toilet). Combination preparations (antibiotic + steroid): widely used; efficacy superior to single agents for OE with inflammation. Cerumenolytics (docusate sodium, olive oil, sodium bicarbonate, hydrogen peroxide): soften and dislodge impacted cerumen → easier removal.
dispensing_guidance: Ear drop instillation: patient warm drops to body temperature (cold drops → vertigo/nausea — caloric effect on vestibular system). Lie on side with affected ear upward. Instil prescribed number of drops → apply gentle pressure to tragus (pump drops inward) → remain lying for 5-10 min for adequate contact. Cotton wool plug after. Otitis externa: topical fluoroquinolone (ofloxacin/ciprofloxacin) drops QID x 7 days (superior and safer than aminoglycoside ± steroid). If severe canal oedema (drops cannot penetrate): insert expandable wick (pope wick/bismuth gauze) and drop onto wick. Keep ear DRY throughout treatment (no swimming; hair wash: cotton wool in ear). AOM treatment: pain relief first (paracetamol ± ibuprofen). Antibiotics in AOM: watchful waiting appropriate in mild cases ≥2 years age (many resolve without antibiotics in 2-3 days). Antibiotic if: bilateral AOM <2 years, severe symptoms (severe otalgia, high fever, systemically unwell), TM perforation, <6 months age, no improvement in 48-72 hrs. Amoxicillin 80-90 mg/kg/day (high dose for penicillin-resistant S. pneumoniae) x 5-7 days. Amoxicillin-clavulanate if no response at 48-72 hrs (covers β-lactamase producers). NEVER instil aminoglycoside drops (neomycin, gentamicin) into ears with perforated TM or grommets (tympanostomy tubes) — cochleotoxicity risk. Fluoroquinolone drops safe with perforated TM/grommets. Cerumen removal: soften with olive oil or sodium bicarbonate drops BID x 1 week → irrigation with warm water (body temperature); do NOT irrigate if history of perforation, previous ear surgery, or cholesteatoma.
